narrative_summary:
The BRCA1 missense change p.Arg504Cys (c.1510C>T) lies within the first nuclear‑localisation signal of the protein (aa 503‑508) and has been reported in a variety of cancer‑related sequencing studies. Clinical reports have most often classified the variant as a variant of uncertain significance (VUS) or as likely benign. In a Norwegian laboratory survey the variant was assigned class 3 (VUS) in 2016 [PMC9636114], while an Australian national review re‑analysed it using ACMG/AMP criteria and concluded a likely‑benign classification [PMC11607406]. A population‑based screening of healthy Han Chinese individuals identified the same change as a VUS in an asymptomatic carrier [PMC10988807], and an evolutionary analysis of BRCA1/2 pathogenic variants listed it as of uncertain significance in ClinVar [PMC8860097]. Similar VUS designations appear in studies of ovarian cancer response to olaparib [PMC5546431] and in a clinical cfDNA assay validation where it was reported as a VUS [PMC6593250].
Functional investigations have not demonstrated a deleterious effect. A nuclear‑cytosol fractionation assay showed that the Arg504Cys protein localises to the nucleus at levels comparable to wild‑type BRCA1 (≈84 % nuclear) [PMC9957003]. Parallel analyses reported no reduction in protein expression or stability for the variant [PMC9957003]. Screening of BRCA1 phosphorylation motifs also failed to reveal any disruption caused by Arg504Cys [PMC3660339].
Population data confirm the presence of the allele in individuals without a known cancer phenotype. The variant was detected in a healthy individual in a Chinese cohort [PMC10988807] and in a lung‑cancer tumor sample at a 6.93 % allele frequency [PMC11184281]; a germline allele frequency of 0.4863 was reported in a breast‑cancer cohort [PMC9580879]. These observations indicate that the change can be present in both non‑cancer and cancer contexts without a clear disease association.
Overall, the accumulated evidence points toward a non‑pathogenic role for BRCA1 p.Arg504Cys. Functional assays show no impact on nuclear localisation, protein stability, or phosphorylation motifs, and population surveys find the variant in healthy carriers. Clinical databases and laboratory classifications remain heterogeneous, ranging from VUS to likely benign, reflecting residual uncertainty. Nonetheless, the preponderance of functional and population data supports a classification of the variant as likely benign, with the caveat that definitive pathogenicity has not been established.

mentions.quoted_text: Somatic mosaicism for DNA copy-number alterations (SMC-CNAs) is defined as gain or loss of chromosomal segments in somatic cells within a single organism. As cells harboring SMC-CNAs can undergo clonal expansion, it has been proposed that SMC-CNAs may contribute to the predisposition of these cells to genetic disease including cancer. Herein, the gross genomic alterations (>500 kbp) were characterized in uninvolved mammary glandular tissue from 59 breast cancer patients and matched samples of primary tumors and lymph node metastases. Array-based comparative genomic hybridization showed 10% (6/59) of patients harbored one to 359 large SMC-CNAs (mean: 1,328 kbp; median: 961 kbp) in a substantial portion of glandular tissue cells, distal from the primary tumor site. SMC-CNAs were partially recurrent in tumors, albeit with considerable contribution of stochastic SMC-CNAs indicating genomic destabilization. Targeted resequencing of 301 known predisposition and somatic driver loci revealed mutations and rare variants in genes related to maintenance of genomic integrity: BRCA1 (p.Gln1756Profs*74, **p.Arg504Cys**), BRCA2 (p.Asn3124Ile), NCOR1 (p.Pro1570Glnfs*45), PALB2 (p.Ser500Pro), and TP53 (p.Arg306*). Co-occurrence of gross SMC-CNAs along with point mutations or rare variants in genes responsible for safeguarding genomic integrity highlights the temporal and spatial neoplastic potential of uninvolved glandular tissue in breast cancer patients.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study detected the BRCA1 R504C variant among other mutations in uninvolved mammary gland tissue of breast cancer patients, without offering any interpretation of its clinical or functional significance.
article_mentions.title: Concurrent DNA Copy-Number Alterations and Mutations in Genes Related to Maintenance of Genome Stability in Uninvolved Mammary Glandular Tissue from Breast Cancer Patients.

mentions.quoted_text: According to the literature, the BRCA1 protein is known to be mainly located in the nucleus, and the two NLS of BRCA1 are located at aa 503-508 and 607-614. To investigate if some of the variants of interest could alter the nuclear localisation of the protein, subcellular localisation was assessed by a nuclear/cytosol fractionation assay. This was especially interesting to evaluate for the variants p.Lys503Arg, **p.Arg504Cys**, p.Arg610Thr and p.Arg612Gly, which are all located within the nuclear localisation sequences. Similar to the BRCA1 WT protein (84% located in the nucleus), all analysed variants were found to be mainly located in the nucleus fraction (Supplementary Figure S3).

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study functionally examined the BRCA1 Arg504Cys missense variant and found no impact on nuclear localisation or protein stability, while clinical tables classify it as VUS/likely‑benign, leading to a suggested re‑classification toward benignity.
article_mentions.title: Functional Analyses of Rare Germline Missense BRCA1 Variants Located within and outside Protein Domains with Known Functions
